purpose: Conformance vectors for wallet-binding architecture. Architecture: JCS stabilizes bytes, action_ref names the action, request_id scopes the attempt (outside canonical preimage), decision_id proves authorization, wallet signature makes the receipt portable cross-framework. Vectors 1-6 are REJECT; vector 7 is PASS.

architecture.wallet_signature_covers: decision_binding_ref hex string — not the preimage, not the action_ref
architecture.request_id_role: scopes the attempt; intentionally outside canonical preimage

error_codes.REPLAY_UNBOUND: Same action_ref reused across requests without a new authorization decision covering the new request_id.
error_codes.PAYLOAD_DRIFT: JCS bytes of args changed after action_ref was computed; recomputed action_ref diverges.
error_codes.NON_CANONICAL_ENCODING: Fields correct but not JCS-serialized; canonical bytes differ from reference implementation.
error_codes.SIGNER_MISMATCH: Wallet signature is cryptographically valid but signer address does not match agent_id in the action_ref preimage.
error_codes.DECISION_UNBOUNDED: Wallet signature covers action_ref bytes only; decision_id is not covered, so authorization is unproven.
error_codes.SESSION_EXPIRED: Request/session binding expired before the terminal receipt was issued; binding is no longer valid.

vectors.name: replay-unbound
vectors.description: Same action_ref is presented under a different request_id without a new decision_id covering the new request. The authorization was granted for request-A, not request-B. A verifier MUST check that decision_id was issued for the presented request_id; reusing a prior authorization is a replay.
vectors.note: request_id changed, decision_id unchanged — the decision did not cover this request.
vectors.rationale: decision_id MUST be bound to the specific request_id it authorized. Reusing a prior decision_id for a new request_id proves authorization for a different attempt, not this one.

vectors.name: payload-drift
vectors.description: Same request_id and decision_id, but the args of the action were modified after action_ref was computed. Recomputing action_ref over the presented args produces a different digest.
vectors.rationale: action_ref covers the exact args at decision time. A verifier MUST recompute action_ref over the presented preimage and reject if the digest diverges from the one covered by decision_id.

vectors.name: non-canonical-encoding
vectors.description: All fields are correct but serialized in framework-native key order (not JCS lexicographic). The canonical bytes differ from the reference implementation, producing a different decision_binding_ref.
vectors.rationale: JCS (RFC 8785) requires lexicographic key order. A verifier MUST recompute from canonical bytes; accepting framework-native order breaks cross-implementation portability.

vectors.name: signer-mismatch
vectors.description: Wallet signature is cryptographically valid — the signature verifies against the signer's public key. But the recovered signer address does not match the agent_id embedded in the action_ref preimage. The receipt is authentic but belongs to a different agent.
vectors.rationale: A verifier MUST recover the signer from the wallet signature and confirm it matches the agent_id in the action_ref preimage. A valid signature from a different key proves nothing about the authorized agent.

vectors.name: decision-unbounded
vectors.description: Wallet signature is valid and signer matches agent_id, but the signature was computed over action_ref bytes only — not over decision_binding_ref. The decision_id is not covered by the signature, so a verifier cannot confirm the action was authorized.

wallet_signature.note: Signed over action_ref, not decision_binding_ref — decision_id is not covered

vectors.rationale: The wallet signature MUST cover decision_binding_ref, which commits to both action_ref and decision_id. Signing action_ref alone proves the agent acknowledged the action but not that a specific authorization decision was granted.

vectors.name: cross-framework-pass
vectors.description: A receipt issued in framework A verifies correctly in framework B using only the canonical bytes and the public key — no shared checkpoint, no trust in either framework's internal state. This is the portability guarantee the architecture provides.
verification_steps: 1. Recompute action_ref from preimage using JCS
2. Recompute decision_binding_ref from {action_ref, decision_at_ms, decision_id} using JCS
3. Recover signer from wallet_signature over decision_binding_ref
4. Confirm recovered signer == agent_id address in action_ref preimage
vectors.rationale: JCS canonicalization + wallet signature over decision_binding_ref is sufficient for cross-framework verification. The canonical bytes are the only shared state needed.

vectors.name: session-expired
vectors.description: The request/session binding expired before the terminal receipt was issued. The decision_id was valid at decision_at_ms, but the session TTL elapsed before the action completed. A verifier MUST check that the terminal receipt timestamp falls within the session validity window.

decision.note: decision taken at 09:58:20Z — within session window

terminal_receipt.note: receipt issued at 10:01:40Z — 100 seconds after session expiry

vectors.rationale: A decision taken within a valid session cannot authorize an action completed after the session expired. The binding between decision_id and the terminal receipt requires the receipt to be issued within the session validity window.
